_doc: Cross-app SSRF: use a Blind SSRF vulnerability in App A's admin panel to force App A's backend to send a request to App B's internal endpoints. Classic BSCP 'two-app' twist - the apps LOOK isolated but the exploit path bridges them. This workflow assumes you've already found the SSRF entry point and have valid admin creds for App A.

steps._comment: STEP 1 - Confirm the SSRF sink is real BEFORE attempting the pivot. Point it at your OAST host with a unique tag; any hit on the OAST log proves App A's backend made the request.
steps.name: confirm_ssrf_via_oast

steps._comment: STEP 2 - Once SSRF is confirmed, pivot to App B's internal endpoint. App A's backend has IP-layer access to App B that you don't from outside. Often the internal endpoint exposes admin functionality without auth because it 'trusts' the source IP.
steps.name: pivot_a_to_b_internal_admin

steps._comment: STEP 3 - Look for an INTERNAL host the SSRF can reach (App B's localhost / private network). PortSwigger sometimes makes you guess internal IPs (192.168.0.0/24 sweep) or hostnames (intranet, internal-api, etc.) - this loop iterates a few common candidates.
steps.name: sweep_common_internal_targets
